questions.question: In Clean Architecture, which element sits at the **center** of the design, with everything else pointing inward toward it?
options: The database schema and ORM mappings
The business logic / domain core
The web framework and the UI layer
The dependency-injection container
questions.explanation: The defining idea is to put business logic at the center and make UI, database, frameworks, and external services point inward toward it. The database, UI, and DI container are all outer details, not the center.

questions.question: Clean Architecture is essentially the same idea as several other named styles. Which set names those equivalents?
options: Onion, Hexagonal / Ports & Adapters
MVC, MVVM, MVP
Microservices, Service Mesh, Event Sourcing
Layered N-Tier, Client-Server, Peer-to-Peer
questions.explanation: Clean Architecture is the same idea as Onion (Palermo) and Hexagonal / Ports & Adapters (Cockburn), all achieved through dependency inversion. MVC/MVVM are UI patterns, and microservices is explicitly contrasted as a different concern.

questions.question: Under the Dependency Rule, an inner layer must never name a type, function, or even a data format defined in an outer layer.
questions.explanation: The rule states that nothing in an inner circle knows anything about an outer circle — not a type, function, or even a data format. That is precisely what keeps the core independent of details.

questions.question: What is the distinction between *layers* and *tiers*?
options: Layers are physical deployment targets; tiers are logical groupings
Layers are logical separations; tiers are physical deployment targets
They are interchangeable terms for the same concept
Layers apply to microservices; tiers apply to monoliths
questions.explanation: Layers are a logical separation while tiers are physical deployment targets. Clean Architecture is about layers and organizes a monolith well; it does not dictate physical tiering.

questions.question: What is permitted to cross a layer boundary in Clean Architecture?
options: Domain entities, so outer layers can reuse business rules
Database rows mapped directly from the ORM
Simple, isolated data structures (DTOs / plain objects)
The DbContext, passed by reference for efficiency
questions.explanation: Only simple, isolated DTOs / plain objects may cross a boundary. Passing entities or DB rows leaks an inner or outer structure and creates a hidden dependency that violates the Dependency Rule.

questions.question: If the Core cannot depend on the database, how does it end up using one without breaking the Dependency Rule?
options: Infrastructure defines the data-access interface and the Core imports it
The Core defines the interface and Infrastructure implements it, wired at runtime via DI
The Core references the ORM directly but only inside a `partial` class
The UI passes an open database connection down into the Core as a parameter
questions.explanation: The Core owns (defines) the abstraction; Infrastructure implements it. The compile-time dependency is inverted toward the Core, and the concrete implementation is injected at runtime via DI in the composition root. If Infrastructure owned the interface, the Core would depend outward.

questions.question: In the diagrams, what does a **dashed** arrow from UI to Infrastructure represent?
options: A compile-time project reference from UI to Infrastructure
An inheritance relationship between UI and Infrastructure types
A runtime-only dependency: DI wiring of concrete implementations at startup
A forbidden dependency that violates the Dependency Rule
questions.explanation: Solid arrows are compile-time dependencies; the dashed UI to Infrastructure arrow is runtime-only — the DI wiring of concrete implementations in the composition root. It is the one permitted UI-to-Infrastructure tie, not a violation.

questions.question: Why does traditional N-Tier make the Business Logic Layer hard to test compared with Clean Architecture?
options: N-Tier forbids the use of dependency injection entirely
Compile-time dependencies run UI to Business to Data, coupling the BLL to data access (and usually a real DB)
N-Tier puts all business rules inside the database as stored procedures
N-Tier has no UI layer, so there is nothing to drive the tests
questions.explanation: In N-Tier the compile-time dependencies flow top-to-bottom (UI to Business to Data), so the BLL is coupled to data access and usually a real database, making isolated testing hard. Clean Architecture inverts that infrastructure dependency.

questions.question: Which of the following belong in the **Application Core** (Core / Domain)? Select all that apply.
options: Entities, aggregates, and value objects
The interfaces that outer layers implement
EF Core `DbContext` and repository implementations
The composition root (`Program.cs`) that wires DI
Domain events and their handlers
questions.explanation: The Core holds entities/aggregates/value objects, domain events and handlers, and the interfaces outer layers implement. The concrete `DbContext`/repositories live in Infrastructure, and the composition root lives in the UI / Web entry point.

questions.question: The optional Use Cases (Application) layer is allowed to depend on Infrastructure so it can call repositories directly.
questions.explanation: The Use Cases layer depends on Core, not Infrastructure. It talks only to Core interfaces; the concrete Infrastructure implementations are injected at runtime via DI.

questions.question: In the Controller -> Interactor -> Presenter -> View flow, the Interactor calls an **Output Port** and the Presenter implements it. Why is this arrangement used instead of the Interactor calling the Presenter directly?
options: It lets the Interactor run on a background thread
So no outer-layer name appears in inner code — the inner circle owns the port, the outer Presenter implements it (DIP at the boundary)
Because Presenters cannot be instantiated without a DI container
It allows control flow to point inward instead of outward
questions.explanation: Control flows outward, but the Interactor (inner) owns the Output Port interface and the Presenter (outer) implements it, so no outer name appears in inner code — Dependency Inversion applied at the boundary. Control flow still points outward; only source-code dependencies point inward.

questions.question: In the runtime request-flow sequence, what does the Use Case (Core) interact with when it needs data from the repository?
options: The concrete Infrastructure repository class, referenced directly
A Core interface, with the Infrastructure implementation resolved by DI
The Database connection object passed from the Web endpoint
A static service-locator call into Infrastructure
questions.explanation: The Use Case talks only to a Core interface; the concrete Infrastructure implementation is resolved by DI. A direct reference to the concrete class or a static service-locator call would violate the Dependency Rule.

questions.question: Which of the following are cited as benefits of Clean Architecture? Select all that apply.
options: The Core can be unit-tested in isolation without a web host or real database
Implementations (DB, email provider) can be swapped without touching business rules
It provides independent per-feature horizontal scaling
Enforceable dependency rules limit the blast radius of a change
questions.explanation: Testability, swappable implementations behind Core interfaces, and separation of concerns that limits a change's blast radius are listed benefits. Independent per-feature scaling is explicitly NOT provided — Clean Architecture organizes a monolith, not microservices.

questions.question: In the ardalis .NET template's project-reference graph, which statement is correct?
questions.code:
Web      --> Infrastructure
Web      --> UseCases
Infra    --> Core
Infra    --> UseCases
UseCases --> Core
options: Core references Infrastructure so it can reach the DbContext
Core references no other project; every other reference points inward toward Core
Web references only Core and never Infrastructure
UseCases references Infrastructure to call repositories directly
questions.explanation: Core references no other project; all references point inward toward Core. Web does reference Infrastructure (for composition-root wiring), and UseCases depends on Core only — not Infrastructure.

questions.question: The 'composition-root leak' refers to the fact that the UI project usually needs a reference to Infrastructure to register implementations at startup. What is the recommended way to contain this leak?
options: Move all Infrastructure types into the Core project
Keep actual use of Infrastructure types confined to `Program.cs` (optionally avoid the reference with assembly scanning)
Reference Infrastructure from Core instead of from the UI
Make every controller depend on the concrete repository to avoid indirection
questions.explanation: The discipline is to keep actual use of Infrastructure types in `Program.cs` (the composition root) only, and the project reference itself can even be avoided via assembly scanning. Moving Infrastructure into Core or having controllers depend on concrete types would violate the Dependency Rule.

questions.question: For a genuinely trivial application, what is recommended instead of full Clean Architecture layering?
options: Jump straight to microservices for future-proof scalability
A single project with folders, or a Vertical Slice organized by feature
Bolt CQRS and a Mediator onto the app anyway for consistency
Put all logic in the database via stored procedures
questions.explanation: For trivial apps the ceremony isn't worth it; a single project with folders or a feature-based Vertical Slice suffices. Adding CQRS/Mediator layering to a small app is called out as over-engineering, and jumping to microservices prematurely is warned against.

questions.question: The ardalis template validates at multiple boundaries. Which statements about that validation strategy are correct? Select all that apply.
options: The domain enforces invariants via encapsulation, throwing on bad input (assuming pre-validated)
Use Cases validate the command/query objects
Web validates the incoming request DTOs
Validation happens only once, exclusively at the Web boundary
Redundant validation across boundaries is considered an accidental smell to remove
questions.explanation: The domain enforces invariants by throwing on bad input, Use Cases validate command/query objects, and Web validates request DTOs. Validation deliberately happens at multiple boundaries — the redundancy is intentional defensive coding, not a smell.

questions.question: How does the ardalis template treat reads versus writes under its CQRS approach?
options: Commands and queries both must go through repositories for consistency
Commands mutate via repositories; queries are read-only and may bypass the repositories
Queries mutate state; commands only read
Both commands and queries bypass repositories and hit the DbContext directly
questions.explanation: Under the template's CQRS, commands mutate state through repositories while queries are read-only and may bypass them (e.g. querying directly for read models). Queries never mutate, and commands do go through repositories.
